bins.name: Study large loads with generation
bins.desc: Studying large loads together with co-located generation helps identify and mitigate network constraints, protecting residential customers from unnecessary upgrade costs.
quotes: Studying large loads together with co-located generation can help identify and mitigate network constraints and protect customers, especially residential customers, from avoidable upgrade costs.

bins.name: Cost relief through demand-side
bins.desc: Distributed resources and demand-side solutions are among the lowest-cost pathways to grid services and can reduce residential customer bills by offsetting interconnection upgrade costs.
quotes: Many utilities, and DOE itself, have recognized that virtual power plants, which aggregate and control a network of distributed assets, have rapidly become among the lowest-cost pathways to new megawatts for the grid.
There is also significant latent capacity and flexibility in existing, readily deployable distributed resources that can be mobilized far faster than new generation or major network upgrades.
Studying large loads together with co-located generation can help identify and mitigate network constraints and protect customers, especially residential customers, from avoidable upgrade costs.

By allowing both durable peak reductions and aggregated distributed flexibility to qualify under Principles 3 and 7, FERC can give large loads more options to meet interconnection requirements quickly, affordably, and reliably.

bins.name: Flexibility as interconnection tool
bins.desc: Large loads should use aggregated distributed energy resources and demand flexibility to meet interconnection requirements. Off-site DERs can deliver equivalent grid benefits as co-located generation and should be evaluated on performance location within the same constrained area, not physical site.
quotes: There is also significant latent capacity and flexibility in existing, readily deployable distributed resources that can be mobilized far faster than new generation or major network upgrades.
Off-site distributed energy resources can deliver the same types of energy services—generation, load reduction, or transmission relief—that on-site assets provide.

Large loads should therefore be allowed to incorporate aggregated front-of-the-meter and behind-the-meter DERs into their interconnection applications to the extent those resources can offset the transmission or generation upgrades triggered by the request.
A long-term commitment to secure and maintain such contributions should be treated comparably to co-located generation for modeling purposes.

FERC should expand its expedited interconnection considerations beyond large load curtailability and co-located generation dispatchability to include appropriately located off-site distributed energy resources that can deliver curtailment or dispatch services under contract.

what matters is equivalent, verifiable performance in mitigating constraints within the same constrained transmission pocket or local grid area, not whether the asset is physically inside or outside the facility fence line.

bins.name: Timely interconnection integration
bins.desc: Efficient large load integration is central to grid growth. Streamlined study processes that incorporate flexibility mechanisms enable quick, reliable, and affordable interconnections.
quotes: enabling the efficient integration of new large loads will be central to the next era of grid growth

FERC should expand its expedited interconnection considerations beyond large load curtailability and co-located generation dispatchability to include appropriately located off-site distributed energy resources that can deliver curtailment or dispatch services under contract.

By allowing both durable peak reductions and aggregated distributed flexibility to qualify under Principles 3 and 7, FERC can give large loads more options to meet interconnection requirements quickly, affordably, and reliably.

bins.name: Demand-side centerpiece
bins.desc: Demand-side solutions, including load curtailment, energy efficiency, and distributed resources, should be at the center of energy planning, not peripheral. Load flexibility is core grid infrastructure.
quotes: We particularly welcome DOE's recognition of load flexibility as core grid infrastructure.

demand-side solutions—whether achieved through large load curtailment, energy efficiency that permanently lowers peak demand, or dispatchable distributed energy resources—belong at the center of energy planning, not at the edges.

bins.name: Off-site DER eligibility
bins.desc: Interconnection rules should allow large loads to incorporate off-site, appropriately located aggregated DERs in applications and expedited processes. This aligns with Order 2222 precedent and allows performance-based evaluation rather than requiring physical co-location.
quotes: Many utilities, and DOE itself, have recognized that virtual power plants, which aggregate and control a network of distributed assets, have rapidly become among the lowest-cost pathways to new megawatts for the grid.
Off-site distributed energy resources can deliver the same types of energy services—generation, load reduction, or transmission relief—that on-site assets provide.

Large loads should therefore be allowed to incorporate aggregated front-of-the-meter and behind-the-meter DERs into their interconnection applications to the extent those resources can offset the transmission or generation upgrades triggered by the request.
A long-term commitment to secure and maintain such contributions should be treated comparably to co-located generation for modeling purposes.

Expanding eligibility to include appropriately located, non-co-located DERs aligns with the principles established in Order No. 2222, which recognizes that DER aggregations can provide dispatchable, reliability-enhancing services that directly affect transmission system needs.

what matters is equivalent, verifiable performance in mitigating constraints within the same constrained transmission pocket or local grid area, not whether the asset is physically inside or outside the facility fence line.

bins.name: Preserve state authority
bins.desc: FERC rules should set regulatory floors, not ceilings, preserving state authority and avoiding preemption of state programs and investments.
quotes: Rewiring America encourages the Commission to remain mindful of the Federal Power Act's cooperative federalism framework. FERC should set floors, not ceilings, and any rules adopted in this proceeding should avoid preempting or impeding state authority.
